_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Grant County, WI. The westernmost county in the cluster, fronting the Mississippi River. Grant County includes the river towns of Cassville and Potosi, deep Driftless coulees, and some of the earliest Euro-American settlement in Wisconsin via the lead mining district. The Mississippi River bluffs are among the most dramatic landscape features in the state.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Driftless Area throughout. Grant County fronts the Mississippi River on the west and is drained by the Wisconsin River and its tributaries on the north. The terrain is the most deeply dissected in the cluster — steep bluffs along the Mississippi rise 500+ feet above the river, and the interior is a maze of coulees and narrow ridges.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 600–1,280 ft. The Mississippi River floodplain is approximately 600 ft. Ridgetops in the interior exceed 1,200 ft. The vertical relief is the greatest in the cluster.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Deeply dissected Driftless uplands dropping steeply to the Mississippi. The bluffs along the Mississippi are composed of St. Peter sandstone, Prairie du Chien dolomite, and Jordan sandstone — a layer cake of sedimentary rock exposed by erosion. The interior is narrow ridgetops with deep coulees. The Platte River, Grant River, and numerous smaller streams have cut through the rock to create one of the most rugged landscapes in southern Wisconsin.

soil_types.description: Loess-derived silt loam on ridgetops. Variable depth depending on ridge width.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Productive on the broader ridgetops but the narrowness of the ridges limited farm size. Grant County farms tended to be smaller than those on the glaciated plains to the east.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Grant County, Wisconsin. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1974.

soil_types.description: Deep alluvial soils in the coulee bottoms and along the Mississippi floodplain. Fertile but flood-prone.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Rich bottomland that produced corn and hay when it was not under water. The Mississippi floodplain farms were among the most productive in the county but subject to devastating floods.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Grant County, Wisconsin, 1974.

major_waterways.name: Mississippi River
major_waterways.role: Western boundary, primary transportation, economic lifeline
major_waterways.description: The Mississippi defines Grant County's western edge, flowing in a broad valley flanked by dramatic bluffs. Before the railroad, the Mississippi was the primary transportation route — lead ore was shipped downriver from Potosi and Cassville to St. Louis and New Orleans. Steamboat landings dotted the riverfront.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Mississippi bluffs in Grant County rose five hundred feet above the river — {ancestor_name} could stand on the bluff edge and look across the greatest river in America to Iowa on the other side, watching steamboats move lead ore downstream the same way the Sauk had watched canoes.

major_waterways.name: Wisconsin River
major_waterways.description: The Wisconsin River forms the county's northern edge, joining the Mississippi near Prairie du Chien. The confluence of the Wisconsin and Mississippi was one of the most important geographic locations in the history of the Northwest — the French portage route from the Great Lakes to the Mississippi passed through here.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Wisconsin met the Mississippi at Prairie du Chien — the French fur traders, the Ho-Chunk, and eventually {ancestor_name}'s people all understood that this confluence was the crossroads of everything that moved through the interior of the continent.

major_waterways.name: Platte River
major_waterways.description: Flows west through the county to the Mississippi, draining the interior lead mining district. Platteville — named for the river — was a lead mining town that later became the site of a state normal school (now UW-Platteville).
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Platte River ran through the heart of Grant County's lead country — {ancestor_name} knew it as the stream that carried mine runoff downstream and powered the mill where the wheat went after the mines closed.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Prairie on ridgetops and south-facing bluffs; dense forest on north-facing slopes; floodplain forest and marsh along the Mississippi; goat prairies on steep south-facing bluffs
vegetation_eras.description: The Mississippi bluffs supported goat prairies — steep, dry, south-facing slopes too exposed for trees, covered in prairie grasses and wildflowers. The north-facing bluffs carried dense forest. The ridgetop prairies were maintained by fire. The Mississippi floodplain was a complex of bottomland forest, marshes, and backwater sloughs teeming with waterfowl.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Mining disturbance near Platteville and Potosi; ridgetop farming expanding; forest thinned for smelting fuel and construction
vegetation_eras.description: The lead mining era consumed forest for smelting fuel at a prodigious rate. As farming replaced mining, the ridgetops were cleared for wheat. The Mississippi bluffs remained largely untouched — too steep for farming or logging.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Dairy farms on ridgetops; reforested coulees; Mississippi bluff forest preserved
vegetation_eras.description: The dairy revolution transformed the ridgetops from wheat to the diversified dairy pattern. The steep coulee slopes reverted to forest. The Mississippi bluffs became increasingly valued for their scenic quality rather than their mineral potential.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: The steamboat landing at Cassville on a July afternoon was the busiest place in Grant County. The river smell — catfish, mud, cottonwood pollen, and the diesel-like odor of the paddle wheel's boiler — mixed with the metallic weight of lead ingots being loaded onto the boat. The bluffs rose five hundred feet above the landing, sandstone and dolomite layers visible in the cliff face like the pages of a book written in rock. Swallows nested in the cliff faces by the thousands, and their darting shadows passed across the dock. The heat reflected off the river into the town. Across the Mississippi, Iowa was a green wall of bluff. The boat's whistle — a sound that carried up every coulee for five miles — announced departures for Galena, Dubuque, and St. Louis, and every farmer within earshot stopped work to listen.
sensory_snapshots.source: Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989). Schafer, The Wisconsin Lead Region (1932).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: From the ridgetop farm five miles east of Cassville, the October view was the longest in the county — west to the Mississippi bluffs, east across three coulee valleys to the next ridge, and north toward the faint smudge of Platteville on the horizon. The corn had been cut and shocked, the shocks standing in rows like small brown tents. The pasture on the coulee slope below was dotted with Holsteins grazing the last green grass before winter. The air was cold at dawn — frost on the pumpkins in the garden, fog in the coulee bottom — but by noon the sun was warm enough to work in shirtsleeves. The smell was leaf smoke, fermented corn from the silo being filled, and the sharp cheese tang drifting up from the factory in the valley below.
sensory_snapshots.source: Apps, Wisconsin Agriculture (2015). Apps, Every Farm Tells a Story (2005).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: The Mississippi flood of 1936 put the Grant County bottomlands under ten feet of brown water. From the bluff above Cassville, the river was unrecognizable — a mile-wide lake of muddy water carrying barn doors, fence posts, dead livestock, and entire trees downstream. The smell was river mud, decomposition, and the raw sewage that every upstream town had dumped into the flood. The bottomland farms were erased — fences buried, topsoil stripped, buildings shifted off foundations. The bluff farms above were dry but helpless, watching the destruction below. The CCC crews who had been planting trees on the bluffs shifted to flood relief, sandbagging the few structures that could be saved.

landscape_changes.change: Lead mining on the Mississippi frontier
landscape_changes.description: Grant County was part of the lead mining district that drew the first significant Euro-American population to Wisconsin. Potosi, on the Mississippi, was one of the largest lead-shipping points. Platteville developed as the interior mining center. The Mississippi River provided the transportation link that made lead mining commercially viable — ore was smelted locally and shipped downriver to St. Louis.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Grant County's lead went down the Mississippi on steamboats — if {ancestor_name} mined here before 1850, the river was as important as the ore, because without it, the lead was just rock in a hill.

landscape_changes.change: Dairy farming replaces mining and wheat
landscape_changes.description: The dairy revolution reached Grant County later than the eastern counties because the rugged terrain made transportation more difficult. Cheese factories and creameries were built at crossroads in the coulee bottoms. The narrow ridgetop farms were small but productive for dairy, and the coulee pastures provided grazing that grain farming could not use.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The ridgetop was too narrow for a big wheat farm but just right for a dairy herd — {ancestor_name}'s twenty cows on forty ridgetop acres produced more income than a hundred acres of wheat ever had.

narrative_hooks: Grant County's Mississippi bluffs were the edge of the world — {ancestor_name} could stand on the sandstone cliff at Cassville and watch the greatest river on the continent carry steamboats, ice, and the commerce of half a continent past the farm.

The coulees in Grant County ran so deep that the morning sun hit the ridgetop farm an hour before it reached the valley floor — {ancestor_name} started chores in daylight while the neighbors in the bottom were still in shadow.

The lead that made Grant County rich in the 1830s went downstream on the Mississippi to St. Louis — by the time {ancestor_name} arrived, the mines were played out, but the stone smelter ruins in the coulee were still warm to the touch on a summer afternoon.

In Grant County, the Mississippi flood decided which farms survived and which ones started over — {ancestor_name} on the blufftop was safe, but the neighbors in the bottomland lost everything when the river decided to be a lake.
source_references: Schafer, Joseph. The Wisconsin Lead Region. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1932.
Nesbit, Robert C. Wisconsin: A History. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2015.
Curtis, John T. The Vegetation of Wisconsin. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1959.
USDA Soil Survey of Grant County, Wisconsin. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1974.
